metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `code_review_result_v2` (and optionally `code_review_result_v1` for backward compat) to the plan's data-access ground truth schema enumeration and to the env_diagnosis_interval pairing spec, so the implementer knows which schema identifies a code-review PASS on the shared REVIEW_RESULT stream.
evidence: Plan: 'pairing matches on the ERROR record's `gate` field (plan_review/code_review/completion) against the next same-gate PASS (a plan_review_result_v2/code-review PASS for those gates'
code_review/sidecar.py:27: SCHEMA = 'code_review_result_v2'
code_review/sidecar.py:31: ACCEPTED_SCHEMAS = ('code_review_result_v1', 'code_review_result_v2')
The string 'code_review_result_v2' appears nowhere in the plan text — only 'plan_review_result_v2' and 'completion_verifier_pass_v1' are named as PASS schema identifiers.

Plan Data-access ground truth: 'A record's kind is read off data.schema / data.verdict: plan_review_result_v2 (verdict PASS/BLOCK/FAIL) with a metrics block; completion_verifier_pass_v1 / _fail_v1; and gate_error_v1' — code_review_result_v2 is entirely absent from this enumeration.
findings.finding: The plan's env_diagnosis_interval gate-pairing description never names `code_review_result_v2` as the schema identifying a code-review PASS record. The REVIEW_RESULT stream carries both plan-review (schema=`plan_review_result_v2`) and code-review (schema=`code_review_result_v2`) records. The plan says pairing matches 'a plan_review_result_v2/code-review PASS' but only `plan_review_result_v2` is named; `code_review_result_v2` never appears in the plan. An implementer of the raw scan pairing logic has no specification for what schema tag constitutes a code-review PASS to pair with a `gate=code_review` ERROR record.
findings.location: Metric definitions — env_diagnosis_interval section; Data-access ground truth section
scenarios: Implementer writes the gate pairing loop, needs to know what schema(s) to accept as a code-review PASS — the plan does not say, so the implementer may accidentally pair a code_review ERROR with a plan-review PASS (wrong gate) or never pair it at all.
AC line 5 tests gate-field match for plan_review but does not cover gate=code_review; the missing schema name makes the code_review case unspecified and untestable as written.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Data-access ground truth' section, extend the schema enumeration to: '`plan_review_result_v2` (verdict PASS/BLOCK/FAIL); `code_review_result_v2` / `code_review_result_v1` (verdict PASS); `completion_verifier_pass_v1` / `_fail_v1`; `gate_error_v1`'. In the env_diagnosis_interval spec, replace 'a plan_review_result_v2/code-review PASS' with 'a `plan_review_result_v2` PASS (for gate=plan_review) or a `code_review_result_v2`/`_v1` PASS (for gate=code_review)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact schema value(s) that constitute a PASS for each gate in `env_diagnosis_interval` pairing: `plan_review_result_v2` for `plan_review`, `code_review_result_v1` or `code_review_result_v2` for `code_review`, and `completion_verifier_pass_v1` for `completion`.
evidence: Plan: 'pairing matches on the ERROR record's gate field (plan_review/code_review/completion) against the next same-gate PASS (a plan_review_result_v2/code-review PASS for those gates'
code_review/sidecar.py:27: SCHEMA = 'code_review_result_v2'; ACCEPTED_SCHEMAS = ('code_review_result_v1', 'code_review_result_v2')
The plan never names 'code_review_result_v2' as the schema to match for code_review gate PASSes
findings.finding: The plan's `env_diagnosis_interval` definition states pairing a `gate_error_v1` with `gate=code_review` matches against 'the next `plan_review_result_v2`/code-review PASS', but never names the code-review PASS schema (`code_review_result_v2`). A raw REVIEW_RESULT scan sees both plan-review and code-review records, and correctly pairing a code-review ERROR with the next code-review PASS requires matching on `schema in ('code_review_result_v1', 'code_review_result_v2')` — not `plan_review_result_v2`. The plan's abbreviated phrase is ambiguous enough to mislead an implementor into only matching `plan_review_result_v2` for all REVIEW_RESULT gates.
findings.location: Metric definitions — env_diagnosis_interval; also Data-access ground truth
scenarios: Implementor checks `schema == 'plan_review_result_v2'` for both plan_review and code_review gates, producing open intervals for all code-review ERROR → PASS pairs.
A fixture testing gate=code_review ENV diagnosis would silently return open intervals even when a PASS exists.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a table or mapping in the Metric definitions section: `{plan_review: schema in (plan_review_result_v1, plan_review_result_v2) with verdict==PASS, code_review: schema in (code_review_result_v1, code_review_result_v2) with verdict==PASS, completion: schema==completion_verifier_pass_v1}`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the schema→gate mapping table the `env_diagnosis_interval` raw scan uses to classify a REVIEW_RESULT PASS by gate (e.g. `plan_review_result_v2` → `plan_review`; `code_review_result_v2` → `code_review`; `completion_verifier_pass_v1` → `completion`) and add an AC fixture that verifies cross-gate REVIEW_RESULT records on the same ticket do not close each other's ERROR intervals.
evidence: plan says: 'pairing matches on the ERROR record\'s gate field (plan_review/code_review/completion) against the next same-gate PASS (a plan_review_result_v2/code-review PASS for those gates'
code_review/sidecar.py:26-27: EVENT_TYPE = 'REVIEW_RESULT'; SCHEMA = 'code_review_result_v2'
plan_review/sidecar.py:480: 'schema': 'plan_review_result_v2'
ABSENCE: plan does not specify the gate→schema mapping table the reader must use to classify a REVIEW_RESULT PASS record as belonging to plan_review vs code_review gate
findings.finding: The env_diagnosis_interval pairing logic must classify REVIEW_RESULT PASS records by gate (plan_review vs code_review) but the plan does not specify the schema→gate mapping needed to do so. Both gates write REVIEW_RESULT events to the same ticket directory (plan_review_result_v2 for plan-review, code_review_result_v2 for code-review), so a raw scan sees them interleaved. The gate_error_v1 record carries a `gate` field, but the plan only says 'pair with the next same-gate PASS' without specifying which data.schema values count as a plan_review PASS vs a code_review PASS — leaving the implementation underspecified.
findings.location: ## Metric definitions — env_diagnosis_interval paragraph
scenarios: A plan_review gate_error_v1 ERROR on a ticket that also has code-review REVIEW_RESULT events: the raw scan finds the next REVIEW_RESULT PASS, but without the schema→gate map the implementation might match a code_review_result_v2 PASS as closing a plan_review ERROR interval (wrong gate pairing).
findings.suggested_fix: Add a mapping in the Approach or Metric definitions section: gate_schema_map = {'plan_review': {'plan_review_result_v2'}, 'code_review': {'code_review_result_v1', 'code_review_result_v2'}, 'completion': {'completion_verifier_pass_v1'}}. The AC already tests plan_review vs completion cross-gate; add a fixture where a code_review PASS arrives between a plan_review ERROR and its plan_review PASS and assert it is not counted as closing the plan_review interval.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the env_diagnosis_interval pairing rule, replace 'code-review PASS' with the explicit accepted schema set: `data['schema'] in ('code_review_result_v1', 'code_review_result_v2')` with `data['verdict'] == 'PASS'` — matching the pattern used for plan_review_result_v2 and completion_verifier_pass_v1.
evidence: Plan: 'pairing matches on the ERROR record's gate field (plan_review/code_review/completion) against the next same-gate PASS (a plan_review_result_v2/code-review PASS for those gates, a completion_verifier_pass_v1 for completion)'
'code-review PASS' is not a schema string — the actual schemas are 'code_review_result_v2' (current) and 'code_review_result_v1' (legacy), defined in code_review/sidecar.py:27,31
plan_review_result_v2 and completion_verifier_pass_v1 ARE named explicitly — the asymmetry is concrete and tool-verified
findings.finding: The env_diagnosis_interval pairing rule names the code-review gate's PASS schema only as 'code-review PASS' in prose, but never provides the actual schema string(s) (code_review_result_v1 or code_review_result_v2) an executor needs to match it in the raw stream. The plan explicitly names plan_review_result_v2 and completion_verifier_pass_v1 for the other two gates, creating an asymmetric specification that leaves the code_review-gate PASS recognition undefined.
findings.location: Metric definitions / env_diagnosis_interval section
scenarios: Executor tries to match a code-review PASS but has no schema string to compare data['schema'] against — guesses 'code_review_pass_v1' or similar and misses real records.
Executor uses only code_review_result_v2 and silently misses v1 legacy records.
findings.suggested_fix: Update the pairing rule to read: 'a plan_review_result_v2 PASS for plan_review, a record with schema in (code_review_result_v1, code_review_result_v2) and verdict==PASS for code_review, or a completion_verifier_pass_v1 for completion.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the sentinel/representation for open (unclosed) `env_diagnosis_interval` records — e.g. `None` (excluded from aggregates) or a dedicated `OpenInterval` object — and update the AC to assert against that exact value.
evidence: Plan: 'an ERROR with no following same-gate PASS is an open (unclosed) diagnosis interval'
AC: 'an ERROR with no PASS yields an open interval' — asserted, but asserted against what value?
ABSENCE: no sentinel type, value, or representation is defined for the open-interval case
findings.finding: Open `env_diagnosis_interval` intervals (ERROR with no subsequent same-gate PASS) are described as yielding 'an open interval', but the plan never specifies the sentinel value returned — None, float('inf'), a dedicated object, or something else. The AC asserts 'an ERROR with no PASS yields an open interval' but cannot be implemented or verified without knowing the expected type/value.
findings.location: Metric definitions — env_diagnosis_interval; Acceptance Criteria line 1
scenarios: An implementer returns None for open intervals; the test asserts it is float('inf'); test fails at merge.
An open interval is included in the average env_diagnosis_interval calculation with an unexpected value, silently skewing the aggregate.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the metric definition: 'An ERROR with no subsequent same-gate PASS in the retained window yields duration=None; open intervals are excluded from any average/histogram aggregation and counted separately as open_interval_count.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the unit of `env_diagnosis_interval` (e.g. float seconds, or milliseconds as int) in both the metric definition and the AC so the assertion `assert dur == expected` is unambiguous.
evidence: 'The interval = PASS `timestamp` − ERROR `timestamp`' — no unit specified
'assert dur == expected' — no unit for `expected` stated
Timestamps sourced from ns-epoch filename prefixes, so raw difference would be nanoseconds; the metric consumer likely expects seconds or milliseconds
findings.finding: The return unit of `env_diagnosis_interval` (seconds, milliseconds, or a raw timedelta/int of nanoseconds) is never stated, yet the AC asserts `dur == expected` — an executor must guess the unit when both implementing and writing the fixture.
findings.location: Metric definitions — env_diagnosis_interval bullet; AC line 'a gate_error_v1 ERROR row paired with a later PASS... yields a diagnosis-interval duration equal to the fixture timestamp delta'
scenarios: Executor implements in nanoseconds; consumer expects seconds — silent 10^9x error in metric values.
Fixture writes timestamps 5 seconds apart; assertion `dur == 5` passes only if unit is seconds, fails silently if nanoseconds.
findings.suggested_fix: Add '(returned as float seconds)' after 'interval = PASS timestamp − ERROR timestamp' and update the AC to '`assert dur == pytest.approx(expected_seconds)`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the registration mechanism: either (a) `registry.py` imports and extends `REGISTRY` from `sidecar_metrics.py`, or (b) `sidecar_metrics.py` appends its specs to the imported `REGISTRY` list, and ensure AC6's assertion imports only `registry.REGISTRY` (not `sidecar_metrics` directly) to prove the integration is wired.
evidence: Plan: 'registered into src/rebar/metrics/registry.py'
src/rebar/metrics/registry.py: REGISTRY is a plain list[MetricSpec] with no import of external spec modules
AC6: 'the metrics register into src/rebar/metrics/registry.py with source="sidecar" (authoritative) — asserted'
findings.finding: The plan says new metrics are 'registered into `src/rebar/metrics/registry.py`' but never specifies the integration mechanism — whether `sidecar_metrics.py` appends to `REGISTRY` at import time, or whether `registry.py` imports specs from `sidecar_metrics.py`. The existing REGISTRY is a plain `list[MetricSpec]` defined at module level. Without specifying which module imports the other, the implementor could create a circular import (sidecar_metrics imports from registry; registry imports from sidecar_metrics) or produce a module that appends to REGISTRY only if explicitly imported elsewhere — leaving the metric absent from callers that import registry directly without importing sidecar_metrics.
findings.location: Files section; Acceptance Criteria AC6
scenarios: sidecar_metrics.py is never imported by registry.py or any init, so REGISTRY is empty of sidecar specs at runtime even though tests that import both pass.
Circular import: sidecar_metrics imports MetricSpec from registry; registry imports from sidecar_metrics — import error at startup.
findings.suggested_fix: Recommend pattern (a): add `from rebar.metrics.sidecar_metrics import SIDECAR_SPECS` in registry.py and extend `REGISTRY += SIDECAR_SPECS`, consistent with the single-ownership pattern the existing registry uses.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the two REVIEW_RESULT payload shapes: specify that the reader reads `data.metrics` for plan_review_result_v2 and `data.coverage.metrics` for code_review_result_v1/v2, or confirm that only plan-review REVIEW_RESULT events are intended for llm_calls aggregation (and document that code-review cost is excluded).
evidence: plan_review/sidecar.py:497: `'metrics': (verdict.get('coverage', {}) or {}).get('metrics', {})` — hoisted to top-level
code_review/sidecar.py build_payload (line 94-112): no top-level 'metrics' key; only `'coverage': verdict.get('coverage', {})`
code_review/finalize.py:186: `coverage['metrics'] = {'llm_ms': ..., 'llm_calls': batch_criteria + agent_calls, ...}` — nested under coverage
Plan quote: 'sum REVIEW_RESULT.metrics.llm_calls (and latency fields) over the range' — does not distinguish the two payload shapes
findings.finding: The plan's claim that 'all llm_calls/latency derivations read REVIEW_RESULT.metrics ONLY' is structurally ambiguous: plan_review_result_v2 stores metrics at the top-level `data.metrics` key (plan_review/sidecar.py:497: `'metrics': (verdict.get('coverage', {}) or {}).get('metrics', {})`), but code_review_result_v2 stores llm_calls inside `data.coverage.metrics` (code_review/sidecar.py build_payload line 106, code_review/finalize.py:186-193). A raw scan that reads `event['data']['metrics']` will silently get an empty dict for every code-review REVIEW_RESULT event.
findings.location: Metric definitions section: 'llm_call_proxy / latency — sum REVIEW_RESULT.metrics.llm_calls'
scenarios: A REVIEW_RESULT event from a code-review gate contributes zero llm_calls to the aggregate even though it has real cost data under coverage.metrics.
cost_per_accepted_change silently under-counts LLM calls when code-review activity is present on the ticket stream.

findings.finding: The first_pass_verification metric definition does not address the edge case where a ticket's first COMPLETION_VERDICT event is a gate_error_v1 (infra outage at the completion gate), which rides COMPLETION_VERDICT but is not a verification attempt — counting it as 'did not pass on first attempt' is incorrect and silently skews the metric downward during infrastructure instability.
findings.location: Metric definitions — first_pass_verification

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC asserting that `llm_call_proxy / latency` correctly sums at least one latency field (e.g., `llm_ms`) from the fixture REVIEW_RESULT records (e.g., 2+3+4 ms = 9 ms) to give the latency half of this metric a covering criterion.
evidence: Plan text: 'sum `REVIEW_RESULT.metrics.llm_calls` (and latency fields) over the range'
AC2 asserts: 'cost_per_accepted_change over a fixture of 3 REVIEW_RESULT runs summing `llm_calls==9`' — no latency assertion.
AC3 asserts: 'the cost derivations read `llm_calls` ONLY from `REVIEW_RESULT.metrics`' — only `llm_calls`, not `det_ms`/`llm_ms`/`total_ms`.
No AC asserts that the summed latency value equals the sum of `det_ms` (or `llm_ms` or `total_ms`) from the fixture records.
findings.finding: The latency sub-metrics (`det_ms`, `llm_ms`, `total_ms`) are described in the plan body as part of the `llm_call_proxy / latency` deliverable, but no AC directly asserts their correct derivation — the latency half of this metric is uncovered work.
findings.location: Metric definitions — '**llm_call_proxy / latency**' bullet; Acceptance Criteria — AC2, AC3 (cover only `llm_calls`)
scenarios: An implementer omits the latency aggregation entirely (returning 0 or None for latency fields) and all ACs still pass, leaving latency silently broken.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the rationale for bypassing `latest_pass_record`/`all_review_results` in favor of a raw ordered scan in a persistent location — either a module-level docstring in `sidecar_metrics.py` or an AGENTS.md/ADR entry — so future maintainers cannot accidentally 'fix' it back to the helpers.
evidence: 'A record's kind is read off data.schema / data.verdict … The raw scan sees ALL of them on the stream in order.'

'The schema-guarded helpers are NOT used for counting, because `latest_pass_record` returns only the single most-recent PASS … and `all_review_results` hard-filters to `plan_review_result_v1/v2` — neither can COUNT completion passes nor surface `gate_error_v1`.'
No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md update, or inline docstring capturing this rationale in the new file.
findings.finding: The plan deliberately bypasses the schema-guarded helpers (`latest_pass_record`, `all_review_results`) in favor of a raw ordered scan — a non-obvious architectural decision whose rationale lives only in the plan document. No ADR, AGENTS.md entry, or inline code comment is specified to capture this decision for future maintainers, who could easily 'fix' the raw scan back to the helpers, silently reintroducing the inability to count multiple completion passes or surface `gate_error_v1` records.
findings.location: Files section / Data-access ground truth section
scenarios: A future maintainer sees the raw scan, considers it fragile compared to the 'proper' schema-guarded helpers, refactors to use them, and silently breaks gate_error_v1 detection and multi-pass counting.
Onboarding a new contributor who doesn't have access to this plan document and can't understand why the helpers are deliberately not used.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a module-level docstring to `sidecar_metrics.py` explaining: 'We use a raw ordered file scan rather than the schema-guarded helpers (`latest_pass_record`, `all_review_results`) because: (1) `latest_pass_record` returns only the single most-recent PASS and cannot count multiple completion passes; (2) `all_review_results` hard-filters to `plan_review_result_v1/v2` and cannot surface `gate_error_v1` records; (3) correct metric derivation requires seeing ALL event types on the stream in chronological order.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement to `sidecar_metrics.py`: for each skipped/unreadable file, emit at minimum a structured log warning (file path, exception type, ticket id) so operators can detect and diagnose data-exclusion events.
evidence: ABSENCE: no mention of logging, counters, or tracing for read-side failures anywhere in the plan
'Pure read-side derivations … Nothing is written' — observability of the read path itself is not addressed
findings.finding: No observability is specified for file-read failures in the sidecar scan. When a file is skipped (corrupt JSON, missing fields), operators have no metric, log line, or alert to know that a ticket's data was excluded from the aggregate.
findings.location: Approach / Data-access ground truth
scenarios: cost_per_accepted_change silently under-counts llm_calls because several REVIEW_RESULT files were silently skipped — no alert fires
An operator sees a sudden drop in first_pass_verification with no way to distinguish 'real decline' from 'data was excluded'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC / unit-test fixture asserting that `llm_call_proxy` returns the correct sum of `REVIEW_RESULT.metrics.llm_calls` (and at least one latency field) over a fixture range.
evidence: Metric definitions: 'llm_call_proxy / latency — sum REVIEW_RESULT.metrics.llm_calls (and latency fields) over the range. source="sidecar"'
Tests section lists cost_per_accepted_change, env_diagnosis_interval, and first_pass_verification fixtures — no llm_call_proxy fixture
Acceptance Criteria: six AC lines — none covers llm_call_proxy or latency sum correctness
findings.finding: The `llm_call_proxy` / latency metric is defined in Metric Definitions and listed as a deliverable but has NO test — not even a happy-path assertion — in either the Tests section or the Acceptance Criteria. Every other named metric has at least one AC line; this one is entirely absent.
findings.location: Metric definitions (llm_call_proxy / latency); Tests section; Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: llm_call_proxy returns wrong sum (off-by-one on range boundary, wrong field name); no test catches it
latency fields summed from wrong key; goes undetected
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'llm_call_proxy over a fixture of 3 REVIEW_RESULT records with llm_calls [2,3,4] and llm_ms [100,200,300] returns llm_calls=9, llm_ms=600 — asserted.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge in the plan that `first_pass_verification` is bounded by COMPLETION_VERDICT's retention cap (10 events per ticket); specify whether open-ended tickets that have exhausted their history should be excluded from the denominator, marked as `Unavailable`, or accepted as best-effort (and downgrade confidence if the latter).
evidence: Plan: 'fraction of tickets whose FIRST COMPLETION_VERDICT record (oldest-first by filename order) is a completion_verifier_pass_v1'
completion_sidecar.py:39: RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 10
completion_sidecar.py:63-91: prune() deletes oldest events beyond keep=10, so the chronologically-first record may have been pruned
ABSENCE: plan does not acknowledge the retention bound or its effect on 'first' record accuracy
findings.finding: The `first_pass_verification` metric can silently return a wrong 'first' record for any ticket that has undergone more than 10 completion attempts, because COMPLETION_VERDICT is pruned to RETAIN_PER_TICKET=10 (completion_sidecar.py:39). The oldest RETAINED record is not the actual first record for heavily-retried tickets, so the metric understates first-pass rate without signalling the staleness.
findings.location: Metric definitions — first_pass_verification; Approach section
scenarios: A ticket with 12 completion attempts where attempts 1-2 (both FAIL) were pruned: the oldest retained record is attempt 3, so the metric scores it as 'first-pass = pass' if attempt 3 passed — wrong.
Batch metrics over a long-lived project where multiple tickets exceed 10 completion attempts silently skew the first-pass rate upward.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the metric definition: 'If a ticket's COMPLETION_VERDICT file-list has been pruned such that the oldest retained record is not the chronologically first (detectable by checking whether the oldest filename's ns prefix is after the ticket's create-event timestamp), exclude that ticket from the denominator or return Unavailable for that data point.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document in the plan that `llm_call_proxy` and `cost_per_accepted_change` are bounded to the RETAIN_PER_TICKET=50 retention window per ticket, and specify the handling when the requested range may exceed that window (e.g. emit a partial-data flag, downgrade confidence, or document as a known scope limitation).
evidence: plan_review/sidecar.py:48: RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 50 — older REVIEW_RESULT events are deleted by prune()
Plan: 'confidence="high"; is_authoritative("sidecar") is True. Nothing is written.'
ABSENCE: plan does not mention the retention window as a scope boundary for these metrics
findings.finding: Both `llm_call_proxy` and `cost_per_accepted_change` are claimed as `confidence="high"` and `is_authoritative("sidecar")=True`, yet they silently undercount whenever the query range spans more events than RETAIN_PER_TICKET=50 (for REVIEW_RESULT), because older events are pruned by plan_review/sidecar.py:prune(). A range query covering, say, 60 review runs will miss the oldest 10, producing a systematically low cost figure with no signal to the caller.
findings.location: Approach section; Metric definitions — llm_call_proxy / latency and cost_per_accepted_change
scenarios: A ticket under active remediation with 55 plan-review runs: the oldest 5 runs' llm_calls are pruned and invisible, making cost_per_accepted_change an undercount with no warning.
A weekly aggregate metric query covering a sprint with more than 50 reviews per ticket silently drops early-sprint data.

findings.finding: The `llm_call_proxy/latency` metric description includes aggregation of latency fields (`det_ms`, `llm_ms`, `total_ms`) over the range, but no Acceptance Criterion verifies the latency aggregation — only the `llm_calls` source constraint is tested (AC#3 via the cost derivation path).
findings.location: Metric definitions ('llm_call_proxy / latency') / Acceptance Criteria

findings.finding: The plan's 'data-access ground truth' section enumerates the REVIEW_RESULT.metrics block as exactly {det_ms, llm_ms, total_ms, llm_calls, claim_path} (citing sidecar.py:496-497), but the actual dict constructed in gate_dispatch.py:281-290 emits a sixth field — verify_requests — not mentioned in the plan. The code comment at sidecar.py:496 echoes only the five fields, so an implementer reading this plan will derive the metrics block shape from the plan's enumeration and may silently omit verify_requests from any passthrough or schema validation in the new sidecar_metrics.py module.
findings.location: Data-access ground truth section — 'plan_review/sidecar.py:496-497' citation; also gate_dispatch.py:281-290
findings.suggested_fix: Update the plan's ground-truth enumeration to: `{det_ms, llm_ms, total_ms, llm_calls, verify_requests, claim_path}` and ensure the sidecar_metrics.py implementation and test fixtures reflect all six fields.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm why a thin extension of the existing `all_review_results` helpers (e.g. accepting `accepted_schemas=None` to mean 'all schemas') cannot replace the bespoke raw scan, rather than duplicating the file-walk boilerplate a fifth time.
evidence: plan_review/sidecar.py:164 — `all_review_results` does: resolve_ticket_dir_name → listdir → sorted → reversed → json.load → schema-guard
code_review/sidecar.py:273 — identical structure for code-review artifacts
completion_sidecar.py:94 and :137 — same loop for COMPLETION_VERDICT

Plan quote: 'A record's kind is read off data.schema / data.verdict … The raw scan sees ALL of them on the stream in order' — this is exactly what a schema-predicate variant of all_review_results would do
findings.finding: The plan proposes a bespoke raw file-walk (tracker_dir + resolve_ticket_dir_name + sorted listdir + json.load) that NIH-duplicates the same loop already implemented four times in the codebase (plan_review/sidecar.py:all_review_results, code_review/sidecar.py:all_review_results, completion_sidecar.py:latest_pass_record, completion_sidecar.py:latest_fail_verdict). The stated justification — those helpers can't count completion passes or surface gate_error_v1 — is a narrow functional gap that could be bridged by a thin schema-accepting variant or a helper that accepts a predicate, rather than re-implementing the full file-walk boilerplate a fifth time.
findings.location: Data-access ground truth section: 'This reader uses a single RAW ordered scan…'
scenarios: Any future schema addition (e.g. a v3) must be updated in five separate scan implementations rather than one.
Error-handling nuances (OSError tolerance, logging posture) already consistent across the four existing loops must be manually replicated again.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `accepted_schemas: tuple | None = None` parameter to the existing `all_review_results` helpers (or a shared `_iter_raw_events(ticket_dir, event_type)` generator) that, when `None`, skips the schema guard and returns every usable event dict; the new reader composes these rather than re-implementing the walk.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the citation in 'Data-access ground truth' from `completion_sidecar.py:29` to `completion_sidecar.py:34` for the `SCHEMA_PASS = 'completion_verifier_pass_v1'` constant.
evidence: completion_sidecar.py:29: SCHEMA = 'completion_verifier_fail_v1'
completion_sidecar.py:34: SCHEMA_PASS = 'completion_verifier_pass_v1'
Plan cites line 29 for SCHEMA_PASS, but that line defines the FAIL schema constant.
findings.finding: The plan cites `completion_sidecar.py:29` for `SCHEMA_PASS = 'completion_verifier_pass_v1'`, but line 29 of that file is `SCHEMA = 'completion_verifier_fail_v1'` (the FAIL schema); `SCHEMA_PASS` is defined at line 34. This is a minor but verifiably incorrect line-number citation in the 'Data-access ground truth' section.
findings.location: Data-access ground truth section — '"Accepted change" is defined precisely: a completion_verifier_pass_v1 record (SCHEMA_PASS, completion_sidecar.py:29)'
scenarios: A reviewer or implementer who spot-checks the plan's citation finds the wrong constant at line 29, undermining trust in the plan's factual accuracy.
findings.suggested_fix: Change '(SCHEMA_PASS, completion_sidecar.py:29)' to '(SCHEMA_PASS, completion_sidecar.py:34)'.

findings.finding: The `first_pass_verification` metric is fully defined in the Metric definitions section but has no acceptance criterion and no test case — no step in the plan verifies it was implemented correctly, leaving the end-state unreachable for this metric.
findings.location: Metric definitions / Acceptance Criteria (gap: first_pass_verification absent from ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: '- [ ] `first_pass_verification` over a fixture of 3 tickets — 2 whose first `COMPLETION_VERDICT` is `completion_verifier_pass_v1` and 1 whose first is a failure — returns 2/3 (asserted as a float within epsilon).' Add the matching fixture + assertion to `test_sidecar_metrics.py`.

findings.finding: The `first_pass_verification` metric (defined in Metric definitions) has no named helper, no raw-scan protocol, and no schema-filter rule specified for retrieving the 'first COMPLETION_VERDICT record' per ticket. The two available helpers (`latest_pass_record` returns the most-recent PASS; `latest_fail_verdict` returns the most-recent FAIL) both return the NEWEST record, not the FIRST/OLDEST. No `all_completion_verdicts` helper exists (verified by search). The plan gives no API path for this metric, unlike the other three which name helpers or scan strategies explicitly.
findings.location: Metric definitions / first_pass_verification

findings.finding: The `first_pass_verification` metric is described in the Metric Definitions section but has no covering Acceptance Criterion; it is an uncovered deliverable.
findings.location: Metric definitions / Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'first_pass_verification over a fixture of 3 tickets where 2 have their first COMPLETION_VERDICT as completion_verifier_pass_v1 returns 2/3; a fixture with 0 tickets returns None — both asserted.'

findings.finding: The `first_pass_verification` metric is defined in Metric Definitions but has no corresponding test fixture, assertion, or Acceptance Criterion anywhere in the plan — it is a new user-facing metric with zero stated test coverage.
findings.location: Metric definitions / Tests section / Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Tests: 'first_pass_verification over 2 tickets where ticket A's first COMPLETION_VERDICT is completion_verifier_pass_v1 and ticket B's first is a fail record returns 0.5; over 0 tickets returns None.' Add a corresponding AC5.

findings.finding: `latest_pass_record` returns only the single most-recent PASS record for a ticket (completion_sidecar.py:137-179), but `cost_per_accepted_change` needs a COUNT of all `completion_verifier_pass_v1` records across all tickets in a time range, and `first_pass_verification` needs the FIRST (oldest) `COMPLETION_VERDICT` per ticket. The plan lists `latest_pass_record(id, repo_root=)` as a 'ready helper' for these derivations, but that API cannot satisfy either requirement — an unverified capability assertion.
findings.location: Data-access ground truth section / Metric definitions (cost_per_accepted_change, first_pass_verification)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the `latest_pass_record` reference for cost/first-pass derivations with an `all_completion_verdicts(ticket_id, repo_root=)` raw scan (analogous to `all_review_results`) that returns all COMPLETION_VERDICT payloads ordered oldest→newest; use schema filtering on the results to count `completion_verifier_pass_v1` records and identify the first verdict schema.

findings.finding: The env_diagnosis_interval raw-scan pairing mechanism is under-specified for the case where both plan_review and code_review gates share the REVIEW_RESULT stream on the same ticket: a code_review gate_error_v1 ERROR followed by a plan_review PASS (or vice versa) would produce a spurious cross-gate diagnosis interval, because the plan says 'next PASS record on the SAME ticket's stream' without specifying that the scan must also filter by the `gate` field present in every gate_error_v1 payload.
findings.location: Metric definitions — env_diagnosis_interval; Data-access ground truth section
findings.suggested_fix: In the env_diagnosis_interval definition, add: 'When scanning REVIEW_RESULT files, the ERROR→PASS pairing must respect the `gate` field of the gate_error_v1 record — match only subsequent records from the same gate (e.g., a plan_review ERROR pairs with the next plan_review_result_v1/v2 PASS; a code_review ERROR pairs with the next code_review_result schema PASS), so a plan_review PASS does not close an open code_review diagnosis interval.'

findings.finding: The `cost_per_accepted_change` computation requires a COUNT of all `completion_verifier_pass_v1` records across all tickets in the time range, but the only named helper `latest_pass_record` (completion_sidecar.py:137-179) returns only the single most-recent PASS record for a single ticket — it cannot enumerate or count across tickets or time ranges. No alternative API, raw-scan protocol, or iteration strategy is specified for this aggregation.
findings.location: Metric definitions / cost_per_accepted_change; Data-access ground truth paragraph 2
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 'Data-access ground truth': 'To count accepted closes in range: walk each ticket dir in the tracker, list all `*-COMPLETION_VERDICT.json` files, filter by timestamp within range, load and filter by `schema == SCHEMA_PASS`; `latest_pass_record` is not used here.' Alternatively name an `all_pass_records(ticket_id)` helper analogous to `all_review_results`.

findings.finding: The `env_diagnosis_interval` raw ordered scan is underspecified regarding which host event stream(s) to scan per ticket: `gate_error_v1` records ride on REVIEW_RESULT for plan/code-review gates AND on COMPLETION_VERDICT for the completion gate (as defined in gate_error_sidecar.py:31-35). The plan says 'raw ordered scan of those event files' without specifying whether the implementer must scan both streams or only one.
findings.location: Data-access ground truth: 'gate_error_v1 (ERROR) rides the host stream (REVIEW_RESULT/COMPLETION_VERDICT) ... ERROR/PASS pairing uses a raw ordered scan of those event files'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the metric definition: 'For each ticket, scan both REVIEW_RESULT and COMPLETION_VERDICT event files; pair each gate_error_v1 ERROR with the next non-ERROR, non-gate_error_v1 record on the SAME file stream (same event type) for that ticket.'

findings.finding: The plan names `all_review_results(id, repo_root=)` as a 'ready helper' for the range-based `llm_call_proxy` metric, but this helper is per-ticket (takes a single `ticket_id`). The plan provides no mechanism or named helper to enumerate tickets within a date/range, leaving the cross-ticket iteration strategy unspecified for the implementer.
findings.location: Approach section / Data-access ground truth: 'ready helpers rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar.all_review_results(id, repo_root=)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete assertion for registry registration, e.g. 'a unit test imports the registry and asserts `registry.get("llm_call_proxy").source == "sidecar"` and `registry.get("cost_per_accepted_change").source == "sidecar"`.
evidence: 'the metrics register into `src/rebar/metrics/registry.py` with `source="sidecar"` (authoritative) — asserted' — 'asserted' is the entire proving command
No test file section describes a registry lookup or import check
The unit test file covers cost, first-pass, and interval fixtures but registry registration is not mapped to a specific test case
findings.finding: The registry-registration AC says it is 'asserted' but does not describe what the assertion looks like — no test command, no dict-key check, no import probe — leaving the proving command implicit.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — last bullet: 'the metrics register into src/rebar/metrics/registry.py with source="sidecar" (authoritative) — asserted'
scenarios: An executor writes a no-op comment in the test file saying '# asserted' and the AC passes review without any runtime check.
Registry integration is broken (metric never callable) but all other unit tests pass — the AC is satisfied on paper.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a test case to `test_sidecar_metrics.py`: `from rebar.metrics.registry import registry; assert registry['llm_call_proxy'].source == 'sidecar'` (adjust key names to match actual registration).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify and add a test for `first_pass_verification` when one or more tickets in the fixture have zero `COMPLETION_VERDICT` files (define whether such tickets are excluded from the denominator or counted as failures, and assert that behavior).
evidence: Metric definition: 'fraction of tickets whose FIRST COMPLETION_VERDICT record is a completion_verifier_pass_v1' — silent on tickets with no COMPLETION_VERDICT records
AC: 'first_pass_verification over a fixture of 3 tickets where 2 have their EARLIEST COMPLETION_VERDICT record...' — all three tickets have completion verdicts; zero-verdict ticket not covered
ABSENCE: no AC or test mentions how tickets with no completion events are handled
findings.finding: `first_pass_verification` is undefined and untested for the empty-path case: a ticket that has REVIEW_RESULT records but zero COMPLETION_VERDICT files. The denominator behavior (include or exclude the ticket) is unspecified and no test covers this boundary.
findings.location: Metric definitions (first_pass_verification); Acceptance Criteria — AC line 4
scenarios: A ticket that was opened and gate-reviewed but never reached the close/completion gate has no COMPLETION_VERDICT file; including it as a failure skews the metric down; excluding it silently changes the denominator — both are valid choices but neither is specified or tested

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the Metric definitions section whether `llm_call_proxy` includes code-review `REVIEW_RESULT` records (schema `code_review_result_v1`/`v2`) or is restricted to `plan_review_result_v2` only, and update the data-access ground truth claim accordingly.
evidence: Plan What: 'an LLM cost proxy (llm_calls, latency) from the plan/code-review sidecars'
Plan Data-access: 'Only REVIEW_RESULT (plan_review_result_v2) carries a metrics block — {det_ms, llm_ms, total_ms, llm_calls, claim_path} (plan_review/sidecar.py:496-497)'
code_review/finalize.py:186-193: code-review also writes coverage['metrics'] = {llm_ms, total_ms, llm_calls, ...}
code_review/sidecar.py:106: `'coverage': verdict.get('coverage', {})` — coverage.metrics flows into the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar payload
findings.finding: The plan's `llm_call_proxy` definition says 'sum REVIEW_RESULT.metrics.llm_calls over the range' from 'plan/code-review sidecars', but the raw REVIEW_RESULT scan will encounter both `plan_review_result_v2` records (which carry `metrics.llm_calls` via `coverage.metrics`) AND `code_review_result_v2` records (which also carry `coverage.metrics.llm_calls` per `finalize.py:189`). The plan never explicitly states whether code-review REVIEW_RESULT records are included or excluded from the `llm_call_proxy` sum. The What section says 'from the plan/code-review sidecars' (inclusion), but the Data-access section only cites `plan_review/sidecar.py:496-497`, creating an ambiguity.
findings.location: Metric definitions — llm_call_proxy / latency; Data-access ground truth
scenarios: Implementor reads data-access section and filters to schema==plan_review_result_v2 only, excluding code-review LLM cost from the proxy.
Implementor reads What section and includes all REVIEW_RESULT records regardless of schema, double-counting or including cost the metric was not designed to cover.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and document an error-handling strategy for the raw file scan in `sidecar_metrics.py`: specify that each file is wrapped in a try/except (JSONDecodeError, KeyError, OSError) with a log-and-skip policy, and assert in tests that a corrupt fixture file causes the remaining tickets' metrics to still be computed correctly.
evidence: 'a single RAW ordered scan of each ticket's *-REVIEW_RESULT.json and *-COMPLETION_VERDICT.json event files … json.load; read event["data"] + event["timestamp"]'
ABSENCE: no try/except, no skip-on-error, no fallback strategy mentioned anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan specifies a raw `json.load` scan of sidecar files but defines no error-handling strategy for file I/O failures (missing files, corrupt/truncated JSON, missing `metrics` block on a `REVIEW_RESULT` record). No graceful-degradation or skip-and-continue behavior is described, so a single bad file could raise an unhandled exception and suppress the entire metric batch.
findings.location: Data-access ground truth / Approach / Files: sidecar_metrics.py
scenarios: A sidecar file is partially written (crash mid-write) — json.load raises JSONDecodeError, metric computation aborts entirely
A REVIEW_RESULT file exists but was written by an older code path lacking the `metrics` block — KeyError propagates uncaught
File permissions change mid-scan — OSError silently kills the metric reader
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap each `json.load` + field access in a try/except block that logs a warning (file path + exception) and skips that record. Add an AC asserting that a fixture with one intentionally malformed file yields correct aggregates for the remaining records.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that metric registration in `registry.py` is idempotent (upsert-by-name, not append) and add an AC asserting that calling the registration function twice does not produce duplicate registry entries.
evidence: 'registered into src/rebar/metrics/registry.py'
ABSENCE: no description of whether registration is guarded against duplicate calls, and no retirement/deregistration path is mentioned
findings.finding: The plan registers new metrics into the shared `registry.py` singleton but does not specify whether registration is idempotent under duplicate module import (e.g., in test suites that reload modules). If the registry appends rather than upserts, duplicate entries could produce double-counted metric values.
findings.location: Files: src/rebar/metrics/registry.py / AC: 'the metrics register into src/rebar/metrics/registry.py with source="sidecar" (authoritative) — asserted'
scenarios: Test harness imports sidecar_metrics twice (e.g., via fixture teardown/setup cycles) — registry accumulates duplicate entries — metric values are doubled
A future refactor re-imports the module at runtime — stale entries accumulate in the registry
findings.suggested_fix: Use a dict keyed by metric name for the registry store, so re-registration is a no-op (or explicit overwrite with a warning), rather than a list append.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove the duplicate `first_pass_verification` bullet and keep only the second (more precise) definition that includes 'oldest-first by filename order'.
evidence: '**first_pass_verification** — fraction of tickets whose FIRST `COMPLETION_VERDICT` record is a `completion_verifier_pass_v1`' (first bullet)

'**first_pass_verification** — fraction of tickets whose FIRST `COMPLETION_VERDICT` record (oldest-first by filename order)...' (second bullet, adds 'oldest-first by filename order' and 'first close-verification attempt')
findings.finding: The `first_pass_verification` metric is defined twice in the Metric definitions section with slightly different wording, creating a conflicting-signals issue that could confuse an executor about which definition is authoritative.
findings.location: Metric definitions — two consecutive 'first_pass_verification' bullets
scenarios: An executor sees two subtly different definitions and must guess which is canonical; the first omits 'oldest-first by filename order' while the second adds it.
findings.suggested_fix: Delete the first `first_pass_verification` bullet; the second one is the complete, authoritative definition.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Merge the two `first_pass_verification` bullet points in Metric definitions into a single authoritative definition that includes the 'oldest-first by filename order' ordering rule.
evidence: First bullet: 'fraction of tickets whose FIRST `COMPLETION_VERDICT` record is a `completion_verifier_pass_v1` (passed on the first verification attempt).'

Second bullet (near-duplicate): 'fraction of tickets whose FIRST `COMPLETION_VERDICT` record (oldest-first by filename order) is a `completion_verifier_pass_v1` (passed on the first close-verification attempt). Reads the raw COMPLETION_VERDICT files per ticket in chronological order and inspects the earliest one's schema.'
findings.finding: The 'first_pass_verification' metric is defined twice in the Metric definitions section — the first bullet is a terse version and the second is a more detailed restatement — creating a near-duplicate definition that could confuse implementers about which formulation governs.
findings.location: Metric definitions — two consecutive bullet points both starting '**first_pass_verification**'
scenarios: An implementer reads the first definition (which omits the 'oldest-first by filename order' ordering rule) and implements a different tie-breaking strategy, producing different results from what AC4 expects.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the first (terse) bullet and keep only the second (detailed) bullet, which specifies the chronological ordering rule required by AC4.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the Tests section to enumerate all six AC-required test cases (including `first_pass_verification` 2/3, gate-field isolation for `env_diagnosis_interval`, and registry `source="sidecar"` assertion) so the Tests section is consistent with the Acceptance Criteria.
evidence: Tests section lists: 'cost-per-accepted-change … returns 9; with 0 accepted returns `None`. An ERROR record paired with a later PASS … yields an interval equal to the fixture timestamp delta; an ERROR with no PASS yields an open interval.'
AC4: 'first_pass_verification over a fixture of 3 tickets where 2 have their EARLIEST COMPLETION_VERDICT record as completion_verifier_pass_v1 … returns 2/3 — asserted numerically'
AC5: 'env_diagnosis_interval pairs a gate_error_v1 ERROR (gate=plan_review) with the next plan_review PASS and NOT with an interleaved completion PASS … asserted'
AC6: 'the metrics register into src/rebar/metrics/registry.py with source="sidecar" (authoritative) — asserted'
findings.finding: The Tests section describes only four specific test cases (cost_per_accepted_change ×2, env_diagnosis_interval ×2) but the Acceptance Criteria section requires additional tests — AC4 (`first_pass_verification` 2/3 fixture), AC5 (gate-field match for env_diagnosis_interval), and AC6 (registry source assertion) — none of which appear in the Tests section, making the two sections inconsistent in scope.
findings.location: Tests section vs. Acceptance Criteria AC4, AC5, AC6
scenarios: A reviewer or implementer uses the Tests section as the canonical test plan and misses writing the first_pass_verification fixture, the gate-field interleaving proof, and the registry assertion — yet the plan's own Tests section does not contradict them doing so.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove the duplicate `first_pass_verification` bullet (the first, less-specific one) and keep only the complete second definition specifying 'oldest-first by filename order'.
evidence: Plan: '**first_pass_verification** — fraction of tickets whose FIRST COMPLETION_VERDICT record is a completion_verifier_pass_v1 (passed on the first verification attempt).'

Plan (second entry): '**first_pass_verification** — fraction of tickets whose FIRST COMPLETION_VERDICT record (oldest-first by filename order) is a completion_verifier_pass_v1 (passed on the first close-verification attempt). Reads the raw COMPLETION_VERDICT files per ticket in chronological order and inspects the earliest one's schema.'
findings.finding: The plan defines `first_pass_verification` twice under Metric definitions with slightly different wording, indicating an unresolved edit artifact. While not a logic error, the duplication leaves ambiguity about which phrasing is authoritative (the second adds 'by filename order' and 'close-verification attempt').
findings.location: Metric definitions — first_pass_verification (both bullet points)
scenarios: Implementor reads only the first definition and misses the 'oldest-first by filename order' clarification, implementing a different traversal order.
findings.suggested_fix: Delete the first `first_pass_verification` bullet; the second bullet already contains all necessary detail.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit schema-guard rule to the llm_call_proxy metric definition: specify that only records with `data['schema'] == 'plan_review_result_v2'` (not `code_review_result_v2`, which carries no top-level `metrics` key) are summed when iterating raw REVIEW_RESULT files.
evidence: Plan: 'sum REVIEW_RESULT.metrics.llm_calls (and latency fields) over the range'
Plan data-access: 'Only plan_review_result_v2 carries a metrics block' — stated only in the ground-truth preamble, never repeated as a schema-guard rule in the metric definition or Approach section
code_review/sidecar.py:94-112: build_payload() returns no 'metrics' key — only 'coverage'
plan_review/sidecar.py:497: 'metrics': (verdict.get('coverage', {}) or {}).get('metrics', {}) — only plan_review_result_v2 has this
Both schemas ride the same REVIEW_RESULT event type (plan_review/sidecar.py EVENT_TYPE='REVIEW_RESULT', code_review/sidecar.py EVENT_TYPE='REVIEW_RESULT')
findings.finding: The llm_calls/latency derivation rule says 'sum REVIEW_RESULT.metrics.llm_calls over the range' but never specifies that only records with schema==plan_review_result_v2 should be included. The raw REVIEW_RESULT stream also contains code_review_result_v2 records (verified: code_review/sidecar.py build_payload has no top-level metrics key), so an executor iterating all *-REVIEW_RESULT.json files will silently skip llm_calls from code-review runs or encounter missing-key errors without a schema-guard rule stated in the metric-definition or approach sections.
findings.location: Metric definitions / llm_call_proxy section; Data-access ground truth paragraph 1
scenarios: Executor writes a raw scan that sums data['metrics']['llm_calls'] over all REVIEW_RESULT files and hits KeyError or None on code_review_result_v2 records.
Executor correctly guards on schema but has no spec-grounded authority to do so, causing a review/test disagreement.
findings.suggested_fix: In the metric-definitions section under llm_call_proxy, add: 'Filter to records where data["schema"] == "plan_review_result_v2" before reading metrics.llm_calls; skip code_review_result_v2 records (they carry no top-level metrics key, only coverage).'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
